text: The square is full and there is music. It must be a festival.
The shops are all shut. It cannot be a normal Monday.
He looks exhausted. He must have walked for hours.
There are no roads here. They cannot have driven.
He might have lost his phone.
The food must be really good.
Look at all those people down by the pier. They are pointing at the water and nobody is moving. Something must be going on. It cannot be a normal Tuesday morning.
You are right, that is strange. The fishing boats have not gone out either. There might have been a storm warning. Or, taking a wild guess, someone could have fallen in.
I do not think so. Nobody looks frightened. Look, they are smiling and filming. It must be something wonderful, not an accident.
Come to think of it, that old man has been standing there since dawn. He must have known this was coming. Odds are he has seen it before.
Good eye. He cannot have just wandered down by chance. And the children would not be here on a school day unless it were something special.
Whales, then. My hunch is that a pod came into the fjord. Let us go and find out before I jump to any more conclusions.
It must be a local holiday.
They must have closed early today.
She might have taken the wrong train.
Odds are it is just around the corner.

Hi Marcelo, it is Solveig. So, the mystery at the harbor this morning. I asked around, and I was completely wrong at first. I thought it must have been a fishing boat in trouble, because of all the people. But no. Somebody spotted a pod of orcas in the fjord, right by the pier. They come in maybe once or twice a winter, chasing the herring, so it is a rare thing. That old man who had been standing there for hours? It turns out he must have known they were coming, because he is a retired whale researcher. Can you believe it. I jumped to conclusions and imagined the worst, and it was something wonderful all along. That is the lesson, I suppose. When you cannot ask, you guess, and half the time the guess is wrong. Anyway, they might still be there tomorrow. Come early. Bye now.

Welcome back to the show. Today, the traveler's sixth sense, and why guessing is a skill worth practising. Here is something that happens the moment you land in a country whose language you do not speak. You become a detective. A shop is shut in the middle of the afternoon. It must be a holiday. Everyone is carrying flowers in one direction. There must be a wedding, or a funeral. A stranger keeps glancing at you and smiling. Odds are you have something in common, or you remind them of someone. You read the signs, you form a hunch, and you act on it. And here is the interesting part. You are wrong a lot. The shut shop was just the owner taking a long lunch. But being wrong is not the point. The point is that guessing keeps you curious and keeps you engaged. So do not be afraid to take a wild guess. Read between the lines. Because a traveler who guesses is a traveler who is paying attention.
Every shop is shut, so it must be a holiday.
They are not here yet; they might have missed the bus.
There are no roads here, so they cannot have driven.
She must have gone home already; the lights are off.
I had a hunch that the ferry would be late.
Do not jump to conclusions before we ask someone.
It must be a local holiday.
They must have closed early today.
She might have taken the wrong train.
Odds are it is just around the corner.
